coredata.dc:title: Magnetic photocatalytic nano-semiconductors prepared from carbon quantum dots compounded with copper ferrate and their application in dye wastewater treatment
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Keren
Qian, Guangyan
Yi, Wende
Tang, Wufei
Liu, Fang
Li, Yong
Yang, Changyi
Xiang, Yuxiang
Yao, Huiqin
coredata.dc:description: To investigate new and potent photocatalytic materials for organic pollutants, in this work, copper ferrate (CuFe2O4) nanoparticles were synthesized by sol-gel combustion method and then combined with the microwave synthesis method to prepare CQDs/CuFe2O4, which was used for the degradation of methylene blue (MB) dye. Firstly, scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL), UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS), and electrochemistry were employed to validate the composite effect of CQDs/CuFe2O4. The MB removal (99.86%) was maximized under the optimal conditions (1.5% CQDs/CuFe2O4, 10 mg/L, pH 7.0). Meanwhile, the recycling performance after magnet adsorption and recovery and the effects of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) dosing (0.5% ∼ 3%), dye concentration (10 mg/L ∼ 30 mg/L), and pH (3.0 ∼ 11.0) on the degradation of MB dye by the CQDs/CuFe2O4 system were comprehensively investigated. Finally, based on the findings of the free radical scavenging experiments, the photocatalytic mechanism is proposed that the high catalytic activity of CQDs/CuFe2O4 is owed to its high specific surface area, narrow bandgap, and multiple active sites. On account of the introduction of the CQDs, the rate of electron mobility is improved and carrier complexation is reduced. In conclusion, the present work provides an effective photocatalytic system for removing organic pollutants by CQDs/CuFe2O4 composites at room temperature under neutral conditions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalyst
Azo dyes
Magnetic recyclable
Electromigration
CQDs/CuFe2O4

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Special Issue on Novel Materials for (Photo)Catalytic and (Photo)Electrocatalytic Wastewater Treatment and Valorization

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The XRD patterns of the samples.
(a) SEM image of pure CuFe2O4; (b) SEM image of CQDs/CuFe2O4; (c-d) TEM image of CQDs/CuFe2O4.
(a) UV-Vis DRS; (b) Tauc plots for estimating bandgap energy; (c) Mott-Schottky (M-S) plots; (d) PL emission spectrum of CuFe2O4, CQDs and 1.5% CQDs/CuFe2O4.
(a) Transient photocurrent responses and (b) EIS Nyquist plots of different proportions of CuFe2O4 and CQDs/CuFe2O4.
(a) Photodegradation of different catalysts under UV–visible irradiation, (b) MB photodegradation reaction kinetics, and (c) MB photodegradation apparent rate constant.

(a) Photodegradation of MB solutions of different concentrations by 1.5% CQDs/CuFe2O4 under UV–visible irradiation; (b) MB photodegradation reaction kinetics, and (c) MB photodegradation apparent rate constant.

(a) Photodegradation of MB solutions of different pH values by 1.5% CQDs/CuFe2O4 under UV–visible irradiation, (b) MB photodegradation reaction kinetics, and (c) MB photodegradation apparent rate constant.
(a) Cycling test of 1.5% CQDs/CuFe2O4 composite catalyst under UV–visible light, and (b) catalytic performance of 1.5% CQDs/CuFe2O4 composite catalyst under different capture agents.
Preparation flow chart of CQDs/CuFe2O4 composite.

head.ce:title: Magnetic photocatalytic nano-semiconductors prepared from carbon quantum dots compounded with copper ferrate and their application in dye wastewater treatment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To investigate new and potent photocatalytic materials for organic pollutants, in this work, copper ferrate (CuFe2O4) nanoparticles were synthesized by sol-gel combustion method and then combined with the microwave synthesis method to prepare CQDs/CuFe2O4, which was used for the degradation of methylene blue (MB) dye. Firstly, scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL), UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS), and electrochemistry were employed to validate the composite effect of CQDs/CuFe2O4. The MB removal (99.86%) was maximized under the optimal conditions (1.5% CQDs/CuFe2O4, 10 mg/L, pH 7.0). Meanwhile, the recycling performance after magnet adsorption and recovery and the effects of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) dosing (0.5% ∼ 3%), dye concentration (10 mg/L ∼ 30 mg/L), and pH (3.0 ∼ 11.0) on the degradation of MB dye by the CQDs/CuFe2O4 system were comprehensively investigated. Finally, based on the findings of the free radical scavenging experiments, the photocatalytic mechanism is proposed that the high catalytic activity of CQDs/CuFe2O4 is owed to its high specific surface area, narrow bandgap, and multiple active sites. On account of the introduction of the CQDs, the rate of electron mobility is improved and carrier complexation is reduced. In conclusion, the present work provides an effective photocatalytic system for removing organic pollutants by CQDs/CuFe2O4 composites at room temperature under neutral conditions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The printing and dyeing industry has a large amount of water consumption, 80% ∼ 90% of which is discharged as printing and dyeing wastewater, and the composition of this dye-production wastewater is complex, toxic, and more difficult to treat [1,2]. Azo dyes (methylene blue (MB), methyl orange (MO), rhodamine B (RhB), etc.) with extreme dyeing properties are the most important pollutants in wastewater discharged from the dyeing and textile industries, and their sp2 hybridization may cause severe damage to human DNA, which can lead to a variety of diseases [3]. MB is a typical representative of azo dyes, which can form complex salts with most inorganic salts and belongs to the third group of carcinogenic substances [4]. Therefore, MB was selected as a modeled pollutant in this study.

Recently, semiconductor photocatalytic technology has widely been applied in the field of azo dye degradation and environmental remediation due to its sustainable, cost-saving, and environmentally friendly approach [5,6]. At present, the research on semiconductor photocatalysts such as TiO2, BiOBr, and BiVO4 has made remarkable achievements [7–12]. With further research, magnetic spinel-type ferrite particles with the general formula MFe2O4 (M = Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, and Cu, etc.) have emerged as new replaceable catalysts due to their stable crystal structure, wide light absorption, and excellent mechanical strength [13]. Especially, CuFe2O4 exhibits promising catalytic activity due to the interaction between iron and copper atoms. However, pure CuFe2O4 nanoparticles have a limited number of active sites and weak electron transfer, which is caused by their easy agglomeration and low conductivity [14].

Previous studies have demonstrated that precious metal doping improves their photocatalytic activity. Wen’s group prepared Ce-, Sn-, and Sb-doped copper ferrate, of which Sb-doped copper ferrate degraded tetrabromobisphenol-A (TBBPA) by 90.1% [15]. Yuvakkumar and co-workers synthesized Ce-doped copper ferrate that degraded up to 88% of RhB within 48 h [16]. Further studies revealed that the composite of metal semiconductors with inorganic carbon materials, such as carbon nanotubes, biochar, carbon fibers, and carbon quantum dots [17–20] can increase the electron mobility rate and thus enhance photocatalytic activity. CQDs (size <10 nm) are a new type of fluorescent nanocarbon with strong chemical inertness and unique photoluminescent electron transfer and photoluminescence properties. In recent studies, carbon quantum dot/nitrogen doped zinc oxide (CQD/N-ZnO) composite materials were prepared to investigate their degradation of malachite green (MG) and inhibition of photo corrosion of ZnO crystals [21,22], as well as their compatibility with three different commercial dyes (MG, MB and fluorescein) [23]. Kumar et al. reported that photocatalytic degradation of MB by CQDs and TiO2 complexes synthesized in different ways, and demonstrated that CQD/TiO2 has the fastest degradation rate (3.6 × 10–2 min–1) for MB [24]. These studies indicate that CQDs can improve the carrier separation efficiency and drive the photocatalyst into visible light response, making them a promising new materials [25].

In this context, CQDs/CuFe2O4 composites with high catalytic properties were obtained by the sol-gel combustion method in the present work. By destroying the azo dye MB under UV–visible irradiation, the photocatalytic performance was assessed. The photocatalytic mechanism was investigated by performing the photocatalytic reaction under different trapping agents. The excellent photoresponse and carrier separation efficiency of CQDs/CuFe2O4 leads to its excellent photocatalytic activity, attributed to its extended light absorption range and efficient electron transfer of CQDs. The large specific surface area of CuFe2O4 can provide more active sites for the catalytic reaction. This innovative study provides an alternative technology for designing environmentally friendly and energy-efficient photocatalysts to remove dye effluents.

A modified sol-gel approach utilizing citric acid interaction was used to create CuFe2O4 magnetic nanoparticles, as described in the literature [26,27]. In 30 mL of deionized water, copper cupric nitrate (Cu(NO3)2•3H2O, 0.01 mol) and ferric nitrate nonahydrate (Fe(NO3)3•9 H2O, 0.02 mol) were dissolved. Then, 4 g of citric acid was added. After ammonia was used to neutralize the pigment, it grew darker. The dark green solution was heated to evaporate (70 ℃), and then dried in an oven (130 ℃, 5 h) to create a colloid. In this procedure, heating the colloid causes it to expand and turn black. The black fork-like substance was left behind following cauterization with anhydrous ethanol. The necessary copper iron oxide was milled and then put in a muffle furnace to cauterize (500 ℃, 3 h).

Using the green microwave approach [28,29], CQDs were made. Urea (CH4N2O, 3 g) and citric acid (C6H8O7, 3 g) were mixed in a colorless solution (10 mL) and microwaved for 5 min (750 W) to produce a dark brown solid, dried and made into a 10 g/L CQDs solution.

100 mg of CuFe2O4 powder and 50 mL of anhydrous ethanol were mixed ultrasonically. 4 mL of diluted silane coupling agent (NH2(CH2)3Si(OC2H5)3: deionized water = 1:1) was then applied and stirred in an oil bath at 60 ℃ for 10 h, followed by centrifugal drying. The obtained solid was re-added with 100 mL of deionized water and ultrasonically dispersed. The configured CQDs solution (0.5 mL, 1 mL, 1.5 mL, 3 mL) was admitted and stirred at 25 ℃ for 8 h. The resulting samples were named to be 0.5% CQDs/CuFe2O4, 1% CQDs/CuFe2O4, 1.5% CQDs/CuFe2O4, and 3% CQDs/CuFe2O4, respectively. Scheme 1.

The photosensitive activity of the samples was assessed by degrading MB in a photocatalytic reactor [30]. The photocatalytic device consists of a light source (xenon lamp, 290 nm ∼ 800 nm), a reactor (double Pyrex glass vial that can be fed with circulating water and sealed with highly translucent quartz), and a magnetic stirrer. In the photocatalytic experiment, 100 mL of MB solution and a specific amount of photocatalyst were first thoroughly mixed by sonication for 3 min, followed by 60 min of dark adsorptive time to attain the adsorption equilibrium, and finally, the light source was turned on. Every 30 min during photodegradation, 4 mL of the solution was syringe-extracted from the reactor, filtered twice with a disposable needle filter, and measured three times for absorbance at 664 nm using a UV-Vis spectrophotometer. The average value was then calculated using blank water as the reference. The equation below was used to calculate the degradation rate (η deg) of MB:

Where C 0 represents the initial concentration of the MB dye and C t represents the MB dye concentration at a specific time t.
η
deg
=
C
0
–
C
t
C 0
×
100 %

A Regulus SU8100 cold field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM) and a FeI Talos F200X transmission electron microscope (TEM) were used for the morphological characterization of the samples. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on a D8 Advance X-ray powder diffractometer. Photoluminescence spectra (PL) were obtained on an F-4600 (Hitachi, Japan). The elemental composition and chemical oxidation states were studied by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS, V-770) was used to obtain the absorbance values of the samples at 200 nm ∼ 800 nm. A UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV–vis, UV3300) was used to locate the photocatalytic activity.

First, the structure of the resulting CQD/CuFe2O4 was verified by XRD. The XRD patterns of CQD/CuFe2O4 photocatalysts with various CQD composite levels of difficulty, as well as pure CQD and pure CuFe2O4 nanoparticles, are contrasted in Fig. 1. As can be noticed that the diffraction peaks of successfully synthesized pure CuFe2O4 NPs (JCPDS:34–0425) at 2θ = 18.319，29.907，34.718，35.861，37.121，43.767，57.827，62.156 and 63.630° are attributed to the (101), (112), (103), (211), (202), (220), (321), (224), and (400) crystallographic planes of spinel-type copper ferrate, respectively [31]. There is no noticeable characteristic peak for amorphous pure CQDs, however, there is a bulge at 2θ = 25.8° that corresponds to the (006) face of carbon (JCPDS:26–1077). The characteristic peaks of CuFe2O4 were all detected for the CQDs/CuFe2O4 complexes, and every single one of them displayed amorphous bulges at about 2θ = 10°, proving that CQDs were successfully incorporated into CuFe2O4 NPs. The most noticeable bulges are 1.5% CQDs/CuFe2O4 and may represent the best CQD composite.

Fig. 2 displays the morphologies of the various materials discovered during concurrent SEM and TEM investigations. According to the morphological comparison of CuFe2O4 and CQDs/CuFe2O4, it can be seen that pure CuFe2O4 is a particle size around 100 nm with uniform particle size. The particle formation is attributed to the soft magnetic properties of CuFe2O4 [32] and is prone to further agglomeration to produce pores (Fig. 2a). In contrast, particles other than CuFe2O4 appear on the surface of CuFe2O4 particles in Fig. 2b, which we believe to be CQDs in the aggregated state. Other than that, the introduction of CQDs does not affect the morphology and structure of CuFe2O4. High resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) tests confirm this conjecture, and CQDs of about 10 nm can be seen distributed on the surface of CuFe2O4 in Fig. 2c. Two different lattice stripes are found in Fig. 2d, where the 0.21 nm dotted stripes belong to the CQDs. A prior study using TEM indicated that the particle size of CQDs was 2.61 ± 0.7 nm when determined using a TEM histogram plot. Our findings are consistent with the previously reported values [33]. The 0.10 nm and 0.16 nm linear stripes belong to the (101) and (112) crystal faces of CuFe2O4, respectively. These results can determine the successful complexation of CQDs on CuFe2O4.

The UV–vis DRS ( Fig. 3a) show the light response characteristics of the samples [34]. As the amount of CQDs compounded increases, the light response of the samples in the UV and partly visible regions showed a trend of increasing and then decreasing. Compared with pure CuFe2O4, 1.5% CQDs/CuFe2O4 exhibited stronger visible absorption with the maximal absorption wavelength around 550 nm. The complexation of CQDs significantly improved the light absorption of CuFe2O4. The associated band gap energy was significantly decreased from 1.15 eV for pure CuFe2O4 to 1.11 eV for 1.5% CQDs/CuFe2O4, as calculated by the Tauc plot (Fig. 3b). These findings imply that the presence CQDs alters their energy band structure, enabling more effective visible light usage and enhancing photocatalytic activity [35]. Mott-Schottky (M-S) plots (Fig. 3c) combined with UV–vis DRS tests can calculate the conduction and valence band (VB) positions of semiconductors [36,37]. The Ag/AgCl flat-band potentials of CuFe2O4, 0.5% CQDs/CuFe2O4, 1% CQDs/CuFe2O4, 1.5% CQDs/CuFe2O4, and 3% CQDs/CuFe2O4 were 1.09 eV, 0.98 eV, 0.58 eV, 0.40 eV, and 0.65 eV, respectively, which were comparable to the standard hydrogen electrode (NHE) of 1.31 eV, 1.20 eV, 0.80 eV, 0.62 eV, and 0.87 eV, respectively. Then the E VB is 2.46 eV, 2.34 eV, 1.93 eV, 1.73 eV, and 1.96 eV, respectively.

Steady-state/transient fluorescence spectroscopy (PL) (Fig. 3d) allows the evaluation of the charge carrier lifetime. The fluorescence intensity of 1.5% CQDs/CuFe2O4 is significantly lower compared to pure CuFe2O4 and CQDs, which indicates enhanced charge transfer and a great suppression of the photogenerated electron-hole pair complexation. In addition, the onset of the photoluminescence wavelength is red-shifted, confirming the narrowing of the band gap. the presence of CQDs increases the ability to trap electrons, thus extending the charge lifetime [38].

The transient photocurrent (TPC) response and electrochemical impedance (EIS) were used to evaluate the transfer and separation of photogenerated charges on the surfaces of different photocatalysts [39]. In 0.1 mol/L sodium sulfate solution, with Ag/AgCl serving as the reference electrode, the transient photocurrent response curves are shown in Fig. 4a. The voltage is set at 0.5 V, and the light and dark conditions alternate at a rate of 50 s/time. It is clear that the 1.5% CQDs/CuFe2O4 electrode has the most stable photocurrent density (6.1 μ), which is also much greater than the photocurrent density of the pure CuFe2O4 electrode (4.3 μ). This suggests that the efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole separation can be increased by composite CQDs. The instability of the produced electrodes may be the reason why the 0.5% CQDs/CuFe2O4 and 1% CQDs/CuFe2O4 electrodes in the curves have lower photocurrent density than pure CuFe2O4. As can be seen in Fig. 4b, the EIS also assessed the charge transfer kinetics of the resultant photocatalysis. The impedance radius of 1.5% CQDs/CuFe2O4 is much smaller than that of pure CuFe2O4, which indicates the low electron-hole complexation rate on the surface of 1.5% CQDs/CuFe2O4 composites. Since the photogenerated electrons of CuFe2O4 particles are easily transferred from the conduction band (CB) to CQDs, leading to the separation of electrons and holes, thus preventing the direct complexation and substantially improving the photocatalytic activity.

Therefore, the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/CuFe2O4 was assessed by the degradation of MB under UV–visible light irradiation, as shown in Fig. 5. Under the solution concentration of 20 mg/L and pH 7.0, the dark reaction was first performed for 60 min to reach the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Then the xenon lamp was turned on, and it was observed (Fig. 5a) that the MB was hardly degraded in the presence of CuFe2O4 only. The degradation of MB by CQDs/CuFe2O4 was almost negligible in the absence of light irradiation. The degradation efficiency of CuFe2O4, 0.5% CQDs/CuFe2O4, 1% CQDs/CuFe2O4, 1.5% CQDs/CuFe2O4, 3% CQDs/CuFe2O4 reached 20.56%, 59.72%, 66.31%, 79.34%, 72.14% after UV–visible light irradiation for 180 min, respectively. The apparent rate constants (Fig. 5c) were calculated from the kinetic curves (Fig. 5b) as 0.00132, 0.00487, 0.00496, 0.00794, and 0.00689, respectively. It is obvious that the improvement of the catalytic performance of CQDs/CuFe2O4 relies on the synergistic effect between CQDs and CuFe2O4. It is noteworthy that the degradation of MB is the first to increase and then decrease with the inclusion of CQDs, and the most prominent degradation effect is observed for 1.5% CQDs/CuFe2O4. The reason for this phenomenon may be the aggregation of CQDs during the photocatalytic process, which would lead to a substantial reduction of the specific surface area and loss of active centers, thus weakening the photocatalytic performance [40].

Figs. 6 and 7 show the control experiments performed to investigate the effect of different concentrations of dye (10 mg/L, 15 mg/L, 20 mg/L, 25 mg/L, 30 mg/L) and pH values (pH = 3.0, 5.0, 7.0, 9.0, 11.0, and 20 mg/L of MB solution) on the performance of 1.5% CQDs/CuFe2O4 for MB removal. As expected, with the increase of the initial concentration of the solution, the transmittance decreased, the active sites were covered by dye molecules, and the catalytic efficiency gradually decreased, but still half of the degradation efficiency could be achieved. For example, for an initial concentration of 10 mg/L MB, 99.86% could be achieved in 180 min, while for an initial concentration of 25 mg/L MB, 55.42% degradation could still be achieved in 180 min. The effect of solution pH on the activity is shown in Fig. 7. Under acidic conditions, the presence of H+ and Cl– competes with the dye cations and •OH and h+, respectively, for the active sites, thus affecting the catalytic reaction. Under alkaline conditions, Fe3+ reacts with •OH, thus producing new substances, which in turn changes the appearance and crystal structure of the catalyst and affects its catalytic performance. The degradation efficiencies of the catalysts were 70.58%, 74.58%, 79.34%, 71.98%, and 69.69% after 180 min at different solution pH values, respectively. Therefore, the 1.5% CQDs/CuFe2O4 catalysts showed the highest photocatalytic activity and degradation rate when the solution pH = 7.0.
(1)
Cl
－
+
h
+
→ Cl •
(2)
Cl
－
+ • OH → •
ClOH
−

The stability and reusability of catalysts are important indicators to examine the performance of catalysts in addition to catalytic activity. Based on the strong magnetic properties of the material, after the catalytic experiment of degrading 10 mg/L of MB solution, the catalyst could be recovered through magnets for the next catalytic cycle to investigate its cyclic stability. As shown in Fig. 8a, the efficiency of photocatalytic degradation of MB only decreased from 99.86% in the 1st cycle to 84.84% in the 5th cycle after 5 cycles. The photocatalytic activity was slightly decreased due to the loss of the recovered sample and the inactivation of some active sites of the catalyst, but the catalytic efficiency was still high [41]. A comparison with other photocatalysts reported in dye wastewater treatment (Table S1) shows that CQDs/CuFe2O4 nanoparticles offered higher degradation rate than other materials. In actual wastewater treatment, magnetic substances can be used for catalyst recovery. Therefore, this catalyst is easy to recover and recycle, which can avoid secondary contamination of the catalyst and greatly save processing costs.

The treatment of dye wastewater mainly relies on the reaction of photogenerated electrons (e–) and holes (h+), a class of carriers with high energy on the surface of photocatalytic materials, with oxygen-containing molecules (O2 and H2O), and a series of free radicals triggered from them, which ultimately lead to the degradation and mineralization of pollutants [42].

To better understand the catalytic mechanism of CQDs/CuFe2O4, p-benzoquinone (BQ), tertiary butanol (TB), and silver nitrate (AgNO3) was used to capture superoxide radicals (·O2 －), hydroxyl radicals (·OH), and electrons (e–), respectively [37]. As shown in Fig. 8b, the greatest effect on the degradation rate of MB was observed for AgNO3, followed by BQ and finally TB, thus it can be determined that e– is the most important active species affecting the photocatalytic activity.

The general mechanism of MB degradation by inorganic photocatalytic material CQDs/CuFe2O4 is shown in Fig. 9. The photocatalytic material absorbs light energy and generates e– and h+ on the solid surface, that is, when the incident light energy is larger than the band gap of the photocatalytic material itself, e– and h+ separate, e– is excited from the valence band to the conduction band, and h+ stays in the valence band. At this time, e– in the conduction band and h+ in the valence band have strong reducing and strong oxidizing properties, respectively. These carriers generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) through redox reaction after contact with oxygen-containing molecules. Finally, different ROS molecules (·O2 –,·OH, H2O2, 1O2) oxidize and reduce the organic pollutants and decompose them into small molecules [43]. After the capture experiments, it can be seen that e– and·O2 – are important active substances in the catalytic reaction, which is because the presence of CQDs makes the electron transfer easier, thus reducing the rate of photogenerated carrier complexation. The more transferred electrons reduce the O2 in the air to·O2 –, which participates in the degradation of organic matter. The other holes directly oxidize the organic matter to degrade it.

CuFe2O4 synthesized by sol-gel combustion method is a nanoscale magnetic particle. CQDs were compounded on the surface of CuFe2O4 by microwave method for photocatalytic degradation. It was found that CuFe2O4 exhibited higher catalytic activity for MB when complexed with CQDs compared to CQDs and CuFe2O4. The degradation of MB (20 mg/L) using 30 mg of 1.5% CQDs/CuFe2O4 resulted in the degradation of 79.34% of MB within 180 min. Complete degradation (99.86%) was achieved for 10 mg/L MB solution. The free radical capture experiments showed that the high catalytic activity was attributed to the facilitation of electron transfer by CQDs. This reduces the rate of electron-hole pair complexation and improves the photocatalytic activity.

The authors greatly appreciate the funding from Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2022M713165), Natural Science Foundation of Ningxia Province of China (2023AAC02039), Science and Technology Innovation Program of Hunan Province (2023RC3189) and Hunan Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (2023JJ60551).

Yang Changyi: Data curation. Tang Wufei: Supervision. Li Yong: Data curation. Liu Fang: Data curation. Yao Huiqin: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision. Yi Wende: Data curation. Shi Keren: Writing – review & editing. Qian Guangyan: Data curation, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2023.111737.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.
